column2: Type the base Linux command that lists directory contents.

column2: Type the Linux command that prints the current working directory path.

column2: Type the base Linux command that moves or renames a file.
column5: Changing only the destination name performs a rename; cp leaves the source in place by creating a copy.

column2: Type the base Linux command that copies a file.
column4: cp duplicates a source at a destination.
column5: Use mv when the source should be moved or renamed rather than retained as a separate copy.

column2: Type the base Linux command that removes files or directories.
column5: Directory removal needs additional conditions or options; the official Objective 1.9 recall target is the base command.

column2: Type the base Linux command that changes read, write, or execute permissions.
column5: Use chown for ownership. Permission modes can be expressed symbolically or with octal notation.

column2: Type the base Linux command that changes a file's owner or group.
column5: Ownership and access mode are separate: chmod changes permissions without changing which user owns the file.

column2: Type the base Linux command that searches text for a pattern.
column4: grep finds matching text in input or files.
column5: Use find to locate filesystem entries by criteria such as filename; use grep when the target is text content.

column2: Type the base Linux command that searches a directory tree for files matching criteria such as a name.
column5: grep searches text content instead. The distinction is pathname search versus content search.

column2: Type the base Linux command that checks a filesystem for logical errors.
column5: The approved notes specify an unmounted or read-only volume for checking and repair; the base-command association is the recall target here.

column2: Type the base Linux command that associates a storage device or filesystem with a directory.
column4: mount makes the filesystem accessible at a mount point.
column5: /etc/fstab contains stored mount definitions; mount performs or displays the active association.

column2: Type the Linux command that changes the current shell to another user until that session is exited.
column5: sudo instead runs a specified command with another user's authority without requiring the shell to remain in that identity.

column2: Type the Linux command placed before another command to run that command with superuser or another user's authority.
column5: This narrower scope differs from su, which changes the shell's user identity until the session is exited.

column4: dnf installs, updates, and removes RPM packages.
column5: Choose the package manager supplied by the distribution; apt and dnf are not assumed to coexist on every Linux system.

column2: Type the base Linux command that manages network interfaces, addresses, and routes.
column4: ip provides network interface and routing functions.
column5: The command includes separate address and route views; this card tests only the official base-command association.

column2: Type the base Linux command that tests IP reachability with ICMP.
column4: ping tests reachability and reports round-trip timing.
column5: Use dig for DNS information and traceroute for path discovery; ping is the reachability tool among these commands.

column2: Type the Linux command named by Objective 1.9 that retrieves data identified by a URL.
column5: The returned data can be displayed, searched, parsed, or used in automation; the exact transfer options are beyond this card's scope.

column2: Type the Linux command named by Objective 1.9 that queries DNS information.
column4: dig requests detailed information from DNS servers.
column5: Use ping for reachability and traceroute for the path. dig is the DNS-specific tool among those commands.

column2: Type the Linux command that maps the network path toward a destination.
column4: traceroute discovers successive hops toward a destination.
column5: Some devices do not return the ICMP responses used for path discovery, and firewalls can filter them.

column2: Type the Linux command used to open the system reference manual for another command.
column4: man displays the requested command's manual page.
column5: Manual pages document command behavior and the available options.

column2: Type the base Linux command that displays or concatenates file contents.
column4: cat sends the contents of one or more files to its output.
column5: cat is useful for quick viewing and combination. Use a text editor such as nano when the task is to modify content interactively.

column2: Type the Linux command that provides a continuously updated process and resource-utilization view.
column4: top displays live process, CPU, memory, and load information.
column5: ps produces a process listing. top is the better match when the prompt emphasizes a live resource view.

column2: Type the base Linux command that lists process information and process IDs.
column5: Use top when a continuously refreshed resource view is required rather than a process listing.

column2: Type the base Linux command that reports storage space used by a file or directory.

column5: df answers how much free space a filesystem has; du helps attribute used space to files and directories.

column2: Type the base Linux command that reports free space for filesystems.
column4: df reports filesystem capacity and free space.
column5: Use du when the question asks how much space a particular file or directory consumes.
